claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 6 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 3 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 3 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital/residential accommodation for 13 weeks (>12) and claimant is not regularly spending money on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 6 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

First, let me read the official guidance to ensure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now I can help assess your eligibility for Child Benefit.

To start, **do you have any children under the age of 16, or children under 20 who are in approved education or training?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do! I have two children - Alex who is 6 years old, and Blake who is 3. They both live with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! It's great to know about Alex (6 years old) and Blake (3 years old), and that they both live with you.

Now I need to ask: **Do you live in the UK?**

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! That's helpful to know.

Next question: **Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent, thank you.

One more question: **Are either Alex or Blake currently in hospital or care for extended periods, or are they fostered by you with the local council paying towards their accommodation or maintenance?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Blake has been in hospital for a while now - about 3 months or so. Alex is fine though, no issues there.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that information. I'm sorry to hear Blake has been in hospital.

I need to understand this situation better. **Are you regularly spending money on Blake while he's in hospital (for example, on clothes, toys, or other items for him)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I haven't been able to afford to spend money on his needs while he's in there.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for being honest about that. I understand this must be a difficult situation.

Just to confirm one final detail: **Are Alex and Blake fostered, or are they your own children (including adopted children)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: They're my own children, not fostered.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.

Let me provide you with my assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on my assessment of your circumstances:

**For Alex:** ✅ **You are eligible** for Child Benefit. Alex is 6 years old, lives with you in the UK, and meets all the standard eligibility criteria.

**For Blake:** ❌ **You are not currently eligible** for Child Benefit. Although Blake is only 3 years old and would normally qualify, he has been in hospital for approximately 3 months (around 12 weeks). Under the rules, Child Benefit usually stops after a child has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks. You could continue to receive it if you were regularly spending money on Blake while he's in hospital, but since you've confirmed you haven't been able to afford to do so, the exception doesn't apply in this case.

**What this means:** You can claim Child Benefit for Alex. If Blake's situation changes - for example, if he comes home from hospital or if you're able to start regularly spending money on his needs while he's there - you should contact HMRC as your eligibility for Blake may change.

I hope this helps clarify your situation. Is there anything else you'd like to know?

reasonings: Alex is 6 years old (under 16), lives with you in the UK (Manchester), and nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him. You are responsible for Alex as he lives with you. Alex is not in hospital or care for extended periods. According to the eligibility rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK. You'll usually be responsible for a child if you live with them. All eligibility criteria are met for Alex.

Blake is 3 years old (under 16) and lives with you in the UK, which would normally make you eligible. However, Blake has been in hospital for approximately 3 months (12 weeks). According to the rules, Child Benefit payments might be affected if your child goes into hospital for more than 12 weeks. After 12 weeks, you're usually no longer entitled to Child Benefit. There is an exception: for children in a UK hospital, you can continue to receive Child Benefit if you regularly spend money on them. However, you have confirmed that you have not been able to afford to spend money on Blake's needs while he's in hospital. Therefore, the exception does not apply, and you would not be eligible for Child Benefit for Blake at this time.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester) with two children: Alex (6) and Blake (3). Nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for either child. For Alex, all standard eligibility criteria are met. For Blake, although he would normally qualify, he has been in hospital for approximately 3 months (around 12 weeks), which is the threshold after which Child Benefit usually stops. The claimant is not regularly spending money on Blake while he's in hospital, so the exception that would allow continued payments does not apply. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex only.
